CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper follows a clear organization (Introduction → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and states the main contribution explicitly in the Introduction (“Our main contribution is to introduce a way to deal with long-range structure…”). Notation for key quantities (Gram matrices in Eq.1, transformed Gramian in Eq.3, and the loss terms in Eqs.2 and 4) is generally well defined and consistent, and the experiments section gives concrete implementation choices (per-layer δ values). However, the provided text contains many duplicated paragraphs and formatting artifacts (repeated blocks of the introduction and figure captions), which interrupt readability and make some passages hard to follow; there are also a few minor presentation issues (inline LaTeX/footnote artifacts and occasional awkward phrasing) that would benefit from cleanup.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a clear, concrete novel modification to Gatys et al.’s texture representation by introducing "transformed Gram matrices" that measure co-occurrences between feature maps and spatially transformed maps (Eq. 3) and by adding a cross-correlation loss Lcc (Eq. 4 / Sec. 3). The idea extends existing CNN–Gram approaches to capture long-range spatial structure (translations and flips, Sec. 4.5) and is explicitly differentiated from prior MRF/GLCM and Gatys-style methods in the text. This is a non-trivial and well-motivated extension that would be of interest to experts, but it builds on an established framework rather than proposing a wholly new problem formulation, so the novelty is notable but not revolutionary.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper presents a clear, technically plausible modification (transformed Gramians defined in Eq.3 and loss in Eq.4) and provides implementation details and qualitative comparisons to the Gatys et al. baseline (Sections 3–4). The mathematical definitions are correct and internally consistent, and the claimed equivalence case (linear activations + one-hot kernels) is reasonable. However, the evaluation is entirely qualitative (visual results, Fig.6–9) with heuristic choices for δ (Section 4.1), no quantitative metrics, no user study or statistical tests, and only limited runtime characterization (∼80% increase claimed), so the experimental methodology is incomplete. Overall the technical approach is sound but the evidence is insufficiently rigorous to fully support all empirical claims.
